---
title: "packages"
format: html
---

# A BIT MORE ON PACKAGES AND WRAP UP

## A BIT MORE ON PACKAGES

- development workflow
- sharing workflow

## HOW TO DEVELOP YOUR PACKAGE

Once you have a basic package structure, you want to have it work as a package.

Do this each time you change something (or start an R session):

install using either:

- **Build - Build and Reload** from menu
- **load_all()** command from command line

This will load your functions in your package that is under development.

You can add additional functions to the R subdirectory; when you do, you need to rebuild and reload (see above).

## A FEW MORE ITEMS

Note: You can edit the DESCRIPTION file to add useful information, particularly if you are going to share this package. It also includes dependencies — other packages that your package depends on (e.g. *tidyverse*) — we will come back to this.

It is useful to keep a Quarto file or R script that lets you run your functions and helps you in development, but this will not be part of your package!

Remember to commit your results as you go and push to **Github**.

## DESCRIPTION FILE DEPENDENCIES

Your package may use other packages. To make sure they are available in your package, you can add them to DESCRIPTION using:

**usethis::use_package(“name”)**

For example:

*usethis::use_package(“purrr”)*

Now your functions can use purrr functions. It is good practice when writing your own packages to explicitly reference the package that any function comes from. For example:

*purrr::map_df(fishes, fish_summary)*

Tidyverse is a bit different since it is not really a single package. To include tidyverse in your package use: **use_tidy_dependencies()**

NOTE these functions will change the DESCRIPTION file -you can look and see how

You can directly edit the following fields in the **DESCRIPTION** file: Authors, Description, Title

## CREATING PACKAGES

- create and link with GitHub
- edit your DESCRIPTION file to add a title and description of your package
- populate with R functions
- run *document()* to create help files
- repeat as needed — add functions as you go, then *build* and *load*
- add package dependencies with ***usethis::use_package(“name”)***, which updates your DESCRIPTION file
- add tests

## ADDITIONAL OPTIONS

- add data
- add testing
- share your package

A good reference; https://r-pkgs.org/

## GIVING THE PACKAGE TO OTHERS

To give the package to others (who will simply be using it):

if your users are comfortable with GitHub, you can use:

*devtools::install_github(“yourname/packagename”)*

This can also be a handy way to share the package with yourself. :)

Alternatively - create a sharable file

- build a source package (see the Build directory)
- this will create a \*.gz file that you can distribute
- the user will then **install** this package (see the installation menu, go to “Install from \*.gz file”) in order to have access to the functions and data in your package

## WRAP UP — WHAT WE LEARNED

**Best Practices**

- reproducibility (track changes to your code)
- modular programming
- always start with a diagram/flow chart — break your task into modules with inputs/parameters and outputs
- testing as you go (with sample datasets) and automated testing
- write clear, informative documentation

## PROGRAMMING SKILLS

- building functions
- generating and storing data; data structures
- repeating/looping
- flow control (if, case statements)
- working with APIs to incorporate data into your workflow
- sharing code with others
  - git — for other developers
  - packages — for your clients

## THINGS WE DIDN’T GET TO (WHAT I’D COVER NEXT)

- working on servers and cloud-based programming
- data cleaning
  - in general, what to look for
  - specialized R tools
- data manipulation in R
  - specialized tools in packages like *purrr* and *tidyverse* for sorting, aggregating, and manipulating data
- command line / terminal window
  - basic Unix commands

## AI

- use what you know to guide your workflow — AI assists, you design!
- use AI for syntax, and to learn about features and techniques you have questions about
- take advantage of what you’ve learned about testing (think about what you can test and how to test functionality)
- use *git* and reproducibility
  - be able to go backwards if AI makes unwanted changes to your project
  - consider tracking your prompts in a complex project

## QUESTIONS?

- feedback helps — please add comments to evaluations: what worked, and suggestions for changes
